metadata.title: The Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack Housing Development
metadata.author: Alexa Richman
metadata.subtitle: A Study Over Time

caption.text: Old Harbor Village, also called the Mary Ellen McCormack Housing Development, was New England’s first public housing development, built in 1936 and opened in 1938. Located in Southie between the Andrew and JFK stops on the MBTA Red Line, the development was built on a formerly unused site of 31 acres.

As of summer 2025, ground broke on a $2 billion renovation plan made possible by the Boston Housing Authority, the developer WinnDevelopment, and an elected tenant association, the Mary Ellen McCormack Task Force. Officials have promised that all current Mary Ellen McCormack residents who are required to relocate temporarily for the Redevelopment Project have the right to return and to be rehoused in an appropriately sized new unit at the Mary Ellen McCormack site.

This is not the housing development’s first renovation project since its opening in 1938. What was the neighborhood like before the construction of Old Harbor Village? What steps have these new renovation projects taken to incorporate features of the old neighborhood into the new one?

This is an 1882 map of the land that Old Harbor Village would eventually be built on. From the 1820s onward, South Boston became home to a large Irish population, although Canadians, Germans, Poles, Lithuanians, and Russian Jews also immigrated to the area. Although many may have been grateful for the opportunity to move into subsidized housing in the early twentieth century, some may have had mixed feelings about the change to their culturally diverse neighborhood.

The housing development sits directly beside Carson Beach. As early as the late 1800s, the Boston sewer system began to divert raw sewage into the Boston Harbor near Moon Island. Most of the Boston coastline became polluted, including Carson Beach. The city began to clean its waters in 1985, yet for much of Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack Housing Development’s history, proximity to the beach was not always desirable.

This is a house on Kemp Street, a few blocks north of Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack, in 1898. This type of house design was common in South Boston and continued to reflect the ways in which houses on the outskirts of the development may have appeared and evolved throughout the twentieth century.

A map of the neighborhood directly north of Old Harbor Village, where the house on Kemp Street was built. Notice its proximity to Telegraph Hill, the tallest hill in South Boston, on which there is a monument tower dedicated to Dorchester Heights and the American Revolution. This neighborhood was evidently one around which many cultures and national celebrations were centered.
This is a 1910 map of the land that Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack would be built on in two decades’ time.

Living New Deal reports that the housing development was built on 31 acres of unused land. However, in reality, not all of that land was unused. Some of it pressed right up against existing neighborhoods, and much of that land was privately owned. The Massachusetts Horticultural Society building was displaced, as were properties owned by John J. Collins, William Cotton, George B. Cushing, Frederick F. Ayer, and others.

Around the time of the public housing project’s construction, the Home Owners Loan Corporation (HOLC), in partnership with FDR, created redlining maps, or color-coded maps of American cities which revealed the “safest” or “riskiest” urban areas. Neighborhoods that were deemed safe investments were given a grade of A and colored green; those that were deemed “hazardous” were given a grade of D and colored red. These maps ultimately reinforced racist stereotypes, as African American, immigrant, and Jewish residents were deemed threats to the stability of home values, and their presence was considered an “infiltration.”

As the Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack housing development was being built in the first few years of practiced redlining, the development is featured in redlining maps. This is the area as it appears on the 1937 redlining map of Boston. An arrow points to the new “South Boston Slum Clearance Project,” or what is to become Old Harbor Village. The map’s use of the word “slum” is significant – usually used to describe informal settlements within cities that have inadequate housing and squalid, miserable living conditions; it is interesting that the redlining maps would call the government-sanctioned housing development a slum or a space for slum-dwellers. The word was most likely used to “other” those that live in or near the subsidized housing development as it was built in a high risk-identified area.

The streets immediately southwest of the development, spanning parts of South Boston and Dorchester, were labeled by the redlining maps as level C8. A grade of C, identified in yellow, meant that the area was “definitely declining” according to the HOLC.
This is the official report of section C8 drafted by the HOLC.

The report’s “Inhabitants” section notes that this area was “infiltrated” by a large Jewish population and that 5% of the area consists of foreign-born residents, largely Irish people. Most residents in the area seem to have worked as clerks, business men, and skilled mechanics. Because the HOLC surveys were conducted during the Great Depression, many families, called “relief families” in the report, received various forms of New Deal-era unemployment and other assistance. The number of families receiving relief in this area was “moderate.”

The “Detrimental Influences” section reports “obsolescence” and “threatening lower class encouragement.” Obsolescence was a racially coded term used to describe older, racially integrated, or Black neighborhoods as unsuitable for investment. Consider the unjust paradox of calling a place obsolete, when diverse communities actively live there and share their cultures with the community.
The streets directly north of the housing development were designated level D10 by the HOLC, meaning that it was considered “hazardous,” one of the highest-risk areas for mortgage lending.
This is the official report of section D10 drafted by the HOLC. The house on Kemp Street, shown earlier, is part of D10.

The report’s “Inhabitants” section notes that there was a large Lithuanian population living in what the HOLC deemed section D10, many of whom were laborers, clerks, and relief families. Many “foreign” families “infiltrated” the area, just as many Jewish families “infiltrated” section C8.

According to the HOLC, “Favorable Influences” include “good transportation, schools, etc. Convenient to diversified employment. Within walking distance of downtown Boston.” “Detrimental Influences” include “very poor property, many houses without baths. Congested area. Vandalism.”
The “Clarifying Remarks” section mentions the housing development that was just finishing construction by the time of this HOLC assessment.
An aerial view of the Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack as it was being built in 1935. Notice its proximity to the railway line, beside which a highway is later built.

The opening of the housing development was not without its controversy. On July 31, 1938, the year Old Harbor Village officially opened, a Boston bank’s unnamed real estate agent complained that four families vacated their previous housing to move into the housing development, despite the real estate agent’s claim that they could afford unsubsidized housing. As New England’s first housing project, Old Harbor Village’s opening prompted locals to consider the needs of their community members and assess whether or not they required government housing support; it asked locals to consider questions of privilege and access.

Evidently, Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack has long centered around ideas of displacement and movement – landowners and businesses had to make way for the development’s construction, and when the project was built, it prompted the movement of local tenants.

According to this Boston Globe article, the development almost exclusively selected families that already have children or will have children soon. Although this approach helps children access basic life necessities such as shelter, warmth, and even food, they are simultaneously excluding those without children. Situations like this highlight how housing projects must navigate difficult questions of access, revealing the uncomfortable truth that helping some groups often means that others will be left without support.

In that regard, much of life at Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack was historically centered around children. Not only did builders want the housing development to be a means of relief for families and children, they wanted it to be a site of childhood. This is an image of the Brownies from Old Harbor Village in 1945.
Two children playing by a fountain on Columbia Road, near the housing project, in 1951.
Children of all ages playing together at Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack in July, 1963.
However, around the same time, some buildings in the area were falling into disrepair. This is a photo of a house near the development taken March 29, 1945.

The housing development was not without its complications; sometimes tenants would cause trouble, as was the case with the two boys who set fire to the Old Harbor Village/Mary Ellen McCormack housing development on December 13, 1950.
Yet instances such as these also gesture to the strong community formed at the housing development, as residents were able to band together and support one another during this hard time.
